message:
fcp-host: record per-invoke budget usage on error paths and add host-state preflight tests

record_invoke_budget_usage previously took a borrowed InvokeResponse
and short-circuited when there were no usage_metrics on it, so any
invoke that failed (either before producing a response or with a
HostError) counted zero against the caller's budget. Rate-limit-style
budgets therefore treated errors as free, which lets a misbehaving or
adversarial caller burn connector capacity by issuing operations that
always fail without ever advancing the request counter.

Changes in invoke_handler and execute_batch_operation:

  - record_invoke_budget_usage now takes Option<&InvokeResponse>; None
    is the "errored before any measurable usage" case.
  - When metrics are absent or empty, synthesize a
    UsageMetric::requests(1) so every attempted invoke contributes at
    least one request against the zone's budget.
  - Both the Ok and Err arms of invoke_handler and
    execute_batch_operation now call record_invoke_budget_usage,
    ensuring error paths are accounted for symmetrically with success
    paths.

Two new tests land with this change:

  - evaluate_live_preflight_host_state_rejection_does_not_track_
    cancellation verifies that when evaluate_live_preflight rejects a
    request because the capability token was revoked by the host
    admin state store, the CancellationController does not retain a
    tracked cancellation entry — closing a leak in which preflight
    failures permanently grew the cancellation map.
  - invoke_handler_propagates_host_state_preflight_rejection
    end-to-end verifies that the handler surfaces the host-state
    rejection to the caller with a reason mentioning the token was
    revoked.

Both tests soft-skip when the compiled fcp-test-connector binary is
not on disk, matching the existing subprocess-backed test pattern.

The diff also absorbs routine rustfmt reflow that a prior swarm
touched (no semantic change).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
